email.badnews.001:
About me: Freelance brand designer, solo, three years in. Values the client relationship but has learned not to over-apologize.
Who this is going to: Tom Okafor, marketing lead at a mid-size coffee roaster, the client on a packaging redesign due Friday. Easygoing but has a launch date downstream of this.
They wrote: "Hey! Just checking we're still on track for Friday's handoff, printer needs files Monday morning."

need to tell tom the packaging files are gonna be tuesday not friday. illustrator was late. offer to send the front labels friday so the printer isn't totally blocked. don't grovel
email.badnews.002:
About me: Office manager at a small veterinary clinic. Has to bump a scheduled surgery because an emergency case, a dog hit by a car, just came in and needs the same OR slot.
Who this is going to: Vera Alvarado, owner of Luna, a cat scheduled for a routine spay this afternoon and a regular Tuesday client of the practice.
They wrote: "Hi, just confirming I'm still dropping Luna off at 1pm today for her spay?"

tell luna's owner we have to push her spay, we had a dog come in hit by a car and need the OR. offer tomorrow morning first thing or thursday. one sorry is fine dont pile them on
email.badnews.003:
About me: General contractor running a kitchen remodel. His electrician found the house's original 1962 wiring behind the wall once demo started, meaning an unplanned panel and wiring upgrade before the wall can close.
Who this is going to: Gloria and Paul Whitaker, homeowners mid-remodel whose budget was already tight, expecting the job to stay on the original $38,000 quote.
They wrote: "Hey, saw the crew pulled out early yesterday, everything okay? Are we still on track for the cabinets going in next week?"

tell the homeowner we found old aluminum wiring behind the kitchen wall thats not up to code, has to get fixed before we close the wall back up. its gonna add about 2800 bucks and push cabinets back a week and a half. just say it straight, dont hide behind passive language like 'issues were discovered'
email.cold.001:
About me: Owner of a two-person web design studio in Austin. Direct, no-nonsense, gets maybe one reply per ten cold emails and knows it.
Who this is going to: Maria Delgado, owner of Ironworks Gym, a local independent gym whose website is visibly outdated (last updated 2019, not mobile-friendly). No prior contact.

write a cold email to the owner of a local gym pitching a website redesign. her site is ancient and breaks on mobile. i want a 15 min call. don't be salesy
email.cold.002:
About me: Owner-operator of a one-van mobile dog grooming business in Denver. Books through word of mouth and Instagram, wants recurring corporate accounts to fill weekday slots that currently sit empty.
Who this is going to: Denise Okonkwo, property manager of The Preserve at Cherry Creek, a 300-unit luxury apartment complex with a large dog park amenity. No prior contact.

cold email to an apartment complex property manager, they have a big dog park. pitching a monthly mobile grooming day for residents as an amenity. one clear ask, keep it tight, don't list every service i offer
email.cold.003:
About me: Independent piano tuner and technician, 20 years in the trade, already tunes for two other private schools nearby. Wants a standing twice-a-year maintenance contract instead of chasing one-off calls.
Who this is going to: Karen Whitfield, head of the music department at Brookline Country Day School, a K-8 private school with six pianos across three practice rooms and the main hall. Found through the school's public staff directory, no prior contact.

cold email to a school music teacher about setting up a twice a year tuning contract for their pianos. i tune for two other schools nearby so i know the drill. get straight to it, no 'hope this finds you well' stuff
email.decline.001:
About me: Founder of a 4-person devtools startup. Friendly but firm on pricing; the product is $500/mo and the only discount ever offered is 10% for annual prepay.
Who this is going to: Dana Kim, procurement lead at a 300-person company, three emails into the conversation, clearly interested but pushing hard on price.
They wrote: "Thanks for the demo yesterday, the team was impressed. Before I take this to finance: we'd need to see 40% off the list price to make this work this quarter. Budget approval on our side takes about two weeks, so the sooner we can lock numbers the better."

reply to dana. no way on 40% but we can do 10% if they prepay annual. keep it friendly, keep the deal moving
email.decline.002:
About me: Owner-operator of a small catering company. Standard policy is no menu changes inside 72 hours of an event because it throws off her prep and shopping schedule.
Who this is going to: Yvette Nair, a client with an anniversary dinner event this Saturday, two days away, who just asked for a last-minute menu change.
They wrote: "Hey! Quick ask, can we swap the salmon and the chicken parm for something else, and can you also add a vegan entree? A few more guests confirmed and some are vegan. Thank you!!"

tell them no on swapping the entrees this late, ive already bought and prepped for salmon and chicken parm. but i can add a vegan side option no problem. be clear about the no, dont apologize a ton
email.decline.003:
About me: Cofounder of a 6-person SaaS company that makes scheduling software for small clinics. Roadmap is fixed for the next two quarters and the team has a firm no-custom-integrations policy after getting burned by one-off builds before.
Who this is going to: Carol Jimenez, office manager at Lakeview Family Dental, a paying customer for 8 months, asking for a custom one-off integration with their specific lab software that no other customer uses.
They wrote: "Our billing coordinator asked if you all could build a direct integration with our lab's software, Fusion Dental Labs. We're the only practice using them locally but it would save us a ton of manual entry. Is that something you could do for us?"

reply to the dental office. no on the custom integration, we cant do one off builds for a single customer's lab software, its not sustainable for a 6 person team. point them to the csv export/import we already have as the workaround. dont ramble about why, just say it
email.followup.001:
About me: Freelance videographer who delivered a wedding video 6 weeks ago. Invoice #47 for $1,800 is now 3 weeks overdue. Second nudge; the first one got no reply.
Who this is going to: Priya and Sam, the couple. Lovely people, chaotic inboxes. Almost certainly not malicious, just disorganized.
I previously wrote: "Hi both! Hope married life is treating you well. Just a gentle reminder that invoice #47 ($1,800) was due on the 14th, link's in my earlier email. Let me know if anything's unclear!"

second followup on the overdue invoice. be warm but firmer this time, give them a friday deadline, offer to split it in two payments if that helps
email.followup.002:
About me: Executive director of a small youth literacy nonprofit. Submitted a $15,000 program grant application six weeks ago to a community foundation and hasn't heard back.
Who this is going to: Angela Reyes, program officer at the Hartwell Community Foundation, who confirmed receipt of the application when it was submitted.
I previously wrote: "Hi Angela, wanted to follow up and see if there's any update on our grant application submitted back in May. Happy to answer any questions on our end. Thanks so much!"

second followup to angela on the grant, first one got no reply. ask when we can expect a decision and if she needs anything else from us. mention our program is supposed to start in september so timing matters. no 'just circling back' stuff, get to it
email.followup.003:
About me: Owner of a small produce farm. Sells weekly to four area restaurants on invoice terms. One restaurant is now three weeks past due on a $640 invoice for July deliveries.
Who this is going to: Chef Louis Marchetti, owner of Bistro Louis, a steady account for two years and generally reliable. This is the first time payment has slipped.

email the chef about the overdue invoice, 640 bucks, three weeks late now. weve worked together two years so no need to be weird about it, just ask when i can expect payment this week
email.internal.001:
About me: General manager of a 45-seat neighborhood restaurant, reporting to the two owners who aren't in the building day to day, after a slow June where sales came in under target.
Who this is going to: Co-owners Frank and Bethany Choi, expecting the monthly numbers update.

monthly update to the owners on june numbers. we did 91.4k against a 104k target, down about 12 percent. two real reasons, the new place two blocks over opened in may and pulled some of our regulars, and our ac was out 4 days mid month which killed a slow week even more. starting a thursday industry night promo in july to try to claw some back. just give them the real picture dont sugarcoat or pad it
email.internal.002:
About me: Part-time administrator for a 200-member community church, updating the volunteer board on the roof repair fund ahead of their monthly meeting.
Who this is going to: The church's 5-person volunteer board, who voted in March to fundraise for a $22,000 roof repair. Board chair Hector Reyes sent the request for an update.
They wrote: "Can you send a quick update on where the roof fund stands before Tuesday's meeting? Board wants to know if we're on track."

update to the board on the roof fund before tuesday. were at 14,200 of the 22k goal, came from the spring pledge drive plus a 2500 memorial gift somebody gave in honor of frank. fall pancake breakfast is already on the calendar and should bring in another 3 to 4k. keep it plain, this isnt a fundraising letter its just an update to people who already know the situation
email.internal.003:
About me: Engineering and ops lead at a 12-person startup, sending a postmortem update to the whole company after a 40 minute outage the day before caused by a bad database migration during a routine deploy.
Who this is going to: The whole company, roughly 12 people, mixed technical and non-technical, some of whom got customer complaints during the outage and want to know what happened.

update to the whole company on yesterdays outage. 40 min down around 2pm, caused by a database migration that locked a table way longer than it should have during a normal deploy. we rolled it back and its fixed, and going forward those migrations run in a maintenance window instead of during deploys. half the company isnt technical so dont drown them in jargon, and put the actual status first not last
email.intro_reply.001:
About me: Founder of a bookkeeping service for restaurants (8 clients, wants 20). Genuinely good at the work, awkward at selling it.
Who this is going to: Jess Alvarez, owner of two taco spots, introduced by mutual friend Danny. Jess was cc'd on the intro and replied '@you, Danny says you're the person for restaurant books. our current setup is a mess of spreadsheets.'
Context: "(Danny) Connecting you two! Jess runs Norte Tacos, two locations and growing fast, and the books are eating her Sundays. You two should talk."
They wrote: "Danny says you're the person for restaurant books. Our current setup is a mess of spreadsheets and I do it all on Sunday nights. What would working with you actually look like?"

reply to jess. explain simply what working with me looks like (monthly close, weekly categorization, i handle sales tax filings) and get a 20 min call on the calendar. thank danny but don't gush
email.intro_reply.002:
About me: Physical therapist running a small outpatient clinic, specializes in runner injuries, wants a referral pipeline from local running groups.
Who this is going to: Curtis Webb, coach of the Denver Distance Collective, a 40-person marathon training group. Introduced by Renee, a mutual client who is both the PT's patient and a member of Curtis's group.
Context: "(Renee) Curtis, meet my PT! She fixed my IT band thing in like 6 weeks and half the group has the same issue. You two should talk."
They wrote: "Renee's been raving about you for weeks. A bunch of my runners are dealing with IT band and shin stuff right now. What would it actually look like if we sent people your way, do you do anything with groups or is it all one on one?"

reply to curtis. answer his actual question, i do 1:1 but can offer his runners a free 15 min injury screen so it's low friction for them to try me. suggest a quick call. thank renee but keep it short, don't gush
email.intro_reply.003:
About me: Owner-operator of a 3-person residential solar installation company. Installs mostly through neighbor referrals since word of mouth converts far better than any ad spend.
Who this is going to: Doug Feldman, owner of a house two doors down from a recent installer job. Introduced by that neighbor, Carla, after Doug saw the panels go up.
Context: "(Carla) Hey, connecting you with Doug down the street, he was asking about my panels over the fence yesterday and I told him you guys were great to work with."
They wrote: "Hi, yeah Carla's system looks great and she said her bill dropped a ton. My roof is a similar pitch to hers I think. Not sure where to even start with this stuff."

reply to doug, he doesnt know where to start so just tell him the next step is a free roof assessment, we can look at pitch and shading in person. ask when works. thank carla for the intro but dont go overboard
email.negotiation.001:
About me: Personal chef who runs private dinner parties and small events, quotes per-person pricing. A repeat client is trying to negotiate a lower per-head rate by pointing to referrals she's sent.
Who this is going to: Sandra Ferreira, a repeat client planning a 12-person anniversary dinner, previously used the chef twice at $95 a head, now asking for $75 a head citing referrals.
They wrote: "We'd love to book you again for our anniversary, 12 people. Given I've sent you two referrals already, could we do $75 a head this time instead of $95? Feels fair given the business I've sent your way."

reply to her about the anniversary dinner. cant do 75 a head, doesnt cover the menu for 12 people at that level. but the referrals are appreciated so ill do 85 a head just for this one as a thank you, not a permanent thing. say the number dont write a paragraph justifying my pricing
email.negotiation.002:
About me: Solo mobile app developer working a fixed-price $18,000 contract to build an iOS app for a small retail client. The client keeps adding requests, now push notifications and a loyalty points system, that weren't in the original scope.
Who this is going to: Ben Ostrowski, owner of a small retail business, currently mid-build on the fixed-price app, who just asked for the two new features to be folded into the current price and timeline.
They wrote: "Can we also get push notifications and a loyalty points system added in? Figure since we're already working together it makes sense to just fold it into what we're doing now."

reply to the client. push notifications and loyalty points are not in the original scope or the 18k price, i can add them for about 4200 more and it adds 2 weeks. say that clearly, dont bury the no under a bunch of 'we love working with you' stuff
email.negotiation.003:
About me: Real estate agent representing a buyer, negotiating the commission split with the listing agent on a co-op deal where the listing agent initially offered a lower-than-standard split.
Who this is going to: Dave Kowalski, listing agent on a $540,000 home sale, offered a 2 percent buyer's-side commission instead of the standard 2.5 percent shown in the MLS listing.
They wrote: "Just flagging our listing shows 2% co-op on this one, seller's tight on the numbers. Wanted to give you a heads up before your client makes an offer."

reply to the listing agent, push back on the 2 percent, standard here is 2.5 and my buyer is preapproved and ready to move fast which is good for the seller. ask straight up if there's room to get to 2.5. were submitting the offer either way just want this settled first. skip the 'hope you're doing well' stuff we talk all the time
email.scheduling.001:
About me: Engineering manager at a 15-person startup, hiring for a backend role. The candidate came through a referral from an existing engineer, Jason.
Who this is going to: Carla Chandrasekaran, backend engineer candidate referred by current employee Jason.

email to a candidate to set up a 45 min tech screen. give her tuesday 2pm or wednesday 10am pacific, or she can suggest her own time. keep it short its just scheduling
email.scheduling.002:
About me: Wedding photographer booked solid on Saturdays through October. Needs to lock an engagement shoot date with a couple before her calendar fills.
Who this is going to: Ellie and Dwayne, an engaged couple who booked their wedding package two weeks ago and now need to schedule the included engagement session.
They wrote: "Hi! We're so excited, just wondering when we should plan the engagement shoot? We're flexible on weekends in September."

reply with two date options for their engagement shoot, sept 12 or sept 19, both around 6pm for golden hour at the botanical garden. mention i need to book the garden permit 2 weeks out so need an answer soon-ish. keep it light and short
email.scheduling.003:
About me: HVAC technician at a small three-truck residential company. Needs to get on customers' calendars before the first cold snap of the season.
Who this is going to: A homeowner whose furnace failed its fall inspection last year and is now due for the annual check before winter.

email a customer that its time for their annual furnace check before it gets cold, i have thursday morning or friday afternoon open this week. just need a yes or a time that works better. dont overdo it its a routine reminder
email.support.001:
About me: Owner of a home-based daycare licensed for 8 kids. Yesterday a substitute helper almost released a child to someone not on the approved pickup list before catching it and calling the parent to confirm.
Who this is going to: Nadine Alvarado, mother of Mia, one of the children involved, upset and emailing to ask what happened and what's being done to prevent it.
They wrote: "I need to understand what happened yesterday. I got a call asking if it was okay for someone I've never heard of to pick up Mia. That's terrifying, what is going on with your pickup process?"

reply to mia's mom. explain what actually happened, the sub didnt recognize the pickup person and called to check before releasing her which is why it didnt go further, but she shouldnt have even gotten that far. saying were adding an id check at the door and retraining the sub before she works again. talk like a person who runs a daycare, not customer service
email.support.002:
About me: Service advisor at an independent auto repair shop. A customer came back a second time with the same squeaky brake noise, two weeks after a brake job.
Who this is going to: Gary Petrosyan, a customer who paid $410 for front brake pads and rotors, noise returned within days.
They wrote: "The squeaking is back, same as before I brought it in. I paid over $400 for this. Kind of frustrating to be honest."

reply to the customer about the squeaky brakes coming back. tell him bring it back in, its covered under our 12 month warranty so no charge, probably a bad pad or a clip that needs adjusting. dont pad it out with a bunch of reassurance, just fix his problem and tell him what to do
email.support.003:
About me: Support lead at a 9-person SaaS company that makes a project management tool for small agencies. A customer's CSV export has been silently dropping the assignee column for about a week due to a bug just identified this morning.
Who this is going to: Lauren Chen, owner of a 6-person creative agency and a customer for over a year, who emailed asking why her exported reports to a client are missing assignee names.
They wrote: "My weekly export to a client is missing the assignee column entirely, has been for at least the last two reports. Is this a known issue? I need this fixed, I look bad sending incomplete reports."

reply to the agency owner about the missing assignee column bug. confirm its real, we found it this morning, fix ships tomorrow. tell her the shared view link has the assignee column and works right now as a workaround so she can use that today. one apology is enough, dont stack them
email.thankyou.001:
About me: Event coordinator at a wedding venue. The venue lost power for two hours during a Saturday reception last month, and the DJ kept the party going acoustic on his own battery speaker until power came back, saving the night.
Who this is going to: Wesley, the DJ who covered for the outage without being asked.

thank you email to the dj who saved our butts during the power outage last month at the martinez wedding. specifically thank him for keeping it going acoustic on his own battery speaker for 2 hours and not making it a big deal in front of the couple. tell him im recommending him by name to every couple from now on. keep it short and real not a form thank you
email.thankyou.002:
About me: Volunteer coordinator for a youth soccer league, 14 teams, ages 6 to 12. A local hardware store owner sponsored new goal nets and jerseys for two teams that couldn't afford them this season.
Who this is going to: Pete Vandermeer, owner of Vandermeer Hardware, who donated roughly $1,400 in equipment.

quick thank you to the hardware store owner who covered nets and jerseys for the two teams that couldnt afford them, about 1400 bucks worth. mention the kids will have his logo on their jerseys all season. keep it actually short, this doesnt need to be a whole letter
email.thankyou.003:
About me: High school English teacher. A parent volunteer, Renata Cole, single-handedly ran the school's used book fair fundraiser for a week, recruiting other volunteers and hauling boxes before and after school, and raised more than double last year's total.
Who this is going to: Renata Cole, the parent volunteer.

thank you note to renata, she basically ran the whole book fair by herself this week, recruited volunteers, hauled boxes before and after school every day. it raised more than double last years total which funds our classroom books for the year. make it personal and specific to what she actually did, not some generic thank you note
essay.argumentative.001:
About me: High school junior on the debate team, writing a straight argumentative essay for an English class assignment, not a debate round.
Who this is going to: Her English teacher, grading the assignment; classmates who will hear some essays read aloud.

argumentative essay for english class on open campus lunch for juniors and seniors. evidence: 3 nearby districts (fairmont, west linn, and cascade) already allow it and their published stats show no rise in tardiness, i surveyed 80 kids at our school and 71 were in favor, and our cafeteria only seats 220 people but we have 340 kids in that lunch wave so its actually overcrowded right now. this is a real school assignment so keep the structure normal but dont pad every paragraph into a rule of three, some of my points only have one or two supports and thats fine
essay.argumentative.002:
About me: Owner of a small independent coffee shop on Main Street, writing out testimony as a submitted essay for a city council meeting on a proposed parking meter rate increase.
Who this is going to: City council members and the public record at the meeting.

write out my testimony as an essay for the city council meeting on the parking meter increase from 50 cents to a dollar fifty an hour downtown. evidence: i tracked my own register for 6 weeks after a similar hike in the next town over back in 2019 and saw foot traffic drop 18% there, my customers average a 22 minute visit so the current hour minimum already covers them fine, and the citys own report says meter revenue only covers 4% of the downtown budget so this isnt actually solving anything. use the real numbers dont just say something vague like this could hurt local businesses, i need the specific numbers in there
essay.argumentative.003:
About me: Third-year electrical apprentice with the local IBEW union, writing an argumentative essay for the union newsletter opposing a proposed cut to state apprenticeship funding.
Who this is going to: Readers of the union local's newsletter; state legislators being made aware of it.

essay for the union newsletter arguing against the states proposed 15% cut to apprenticeship funding. use real numbers: my cohort started with 24 apprentices, 21 finished and are working journeyman jobs now, apprentices start around 19 an hour and top out around 46 an hour as journeymen so people are off assistance programs fast, and the total public cost per apprentice who finishes is about 14k versus something like 48k average public subsidy per four year grad who finishes. dont dress this up with big words, im an electrician not an english professor, keep it plain and direct
essay.blog.001:
About me: Owner of a family plumbing company in Minneapolis, writing for the company blog. The posts exist to rank locally and to actually help homeowners; he dictates drafts between jobs.
Who this is going to: Minneapolis homeowners googling how to winterize outdoor pipes.

blog post for our plumbing company: how to winterize your outside spigots. steps: shut interior valve, drain spigot, disconnect hoses, $3 foam cover. the big mistake is leaving hoses connected, that's most of our january burst-pipe calls. tell people to call us if they don't have an interior shutoff. get straight into it, no 'winter is coming' intro
essay.blog.002:
About me: Owner-operator of a small residential HVAC company, writes how-to posts for the company site himself between jobs.
Who this is going to: Homeowners searching for help with cold baseboard radiators, local to his service area.

how to blog post for the company site, how to bleed air out of baseboard heating before winter. steps: shut off the circulator/boiler first, find the bleeder valve on the end of each baseboard unit, cup underneath it, open slow with a key or flathead till the water comes out steady with no hissing air, close it snug but dont overtighten or youll crack the valve seat, go unit by unit starting closest to the boiler and working out. the mistake that causes most of our cold radiator calls is people only bleed the upstairs units and skip the basement loop which is usually where the air actually is. get straight into the steps, no as the weather turns colder intro and dont just restate the title as the first sentence either
essay.blog.003:
About me: Owner-operator of a small dog grooming salon, writes how-to posts for the salon site herself.
Who this is going to: Dog owners, mostly local clients, searching for help with coat matting between grooming visits.

how to post for the grooming salon site about keeping a dogs coat from matting between visits, mainly for double coated and curly breeds. steps: brush before the bath at home never after, wet matted fur just turns into a felt pad, use a slicker brush with short line strokes not circles, work in sections starting behind the ears and under the front legs bc thats where mats start first, if you already see a mat forming work conditioner into it with your fingers before you ever touch it with a brush or comb. the mistake i see constantly is people bathing the dog first thinking itll loosen the mats, it actually locks them in tighter. make it read like im talking not a numbered listicle with a header over every step, and dont restate the title as your first line either
essay.blogpost.001:
About me: Owner of a three-person independent bakery, writing a post for the shop's own blog and newsletter. Bakes starting at 4am most days and writes the blog herself in whatever time is left.
Who this is going to: The bakery's blog and email subscribers, mostly regular local customers.

blog post for the bakery site explaining why were not doing custom wedding cakes anymore. reasons: each order eats about 14 hours of free consult and tasting time, we had 3 last minute cancellations this year that ate the ingredient cost, and it was pulling us away from the everyday case which is what actually pays rent. want it warm not defensive, and please dont turn it into some faq with headers and bullet points, it should just read like me talking
essay.blogpost.002:
About me: Solo indie game developer, three years of nights and weekends work on his first commercial release. Writes a devlog blog that a few hundred people follow.
Who this is going to: His devlog and blog subscribers, mostly other indie developers and early players.

devlog post about launching my game last week. sold 412 copies in the first week, hit the front page of a small subreddit for like 6 hours, one review said the movement feels floaty and honestly theyre right so im patching that in 1.1 next month. dont wrap it up with some big lesson learned thing, i just want to say what happened and whats next
essay.blogpost.003:
About me: Freelance wedding photographer, six years shooting mostly in one metro area, writing a post for her own studio website aimed at couples deciding when to book.
Who this is going to: Couples browsing her photography website, deciding when to book their wedding.

blog post for my site about why i actually like shooting winter weddings now. facts to use: winter bookings run about 30% cheaper for the couple but ive shot 11 of them the last two years, the light through bare trees around 3pm in november is honestly better for portraits than flat overcast june light, and im not sharing the parking lot with 3 or 4 other vendors like i am in june. keep the specifics, dont just turn it into weddings are beautiful year round mush
essay.commentary.001:
About me: Local AM sports radio host and part-time newspaper sports columnist in a small town, writing a short commentary column after the high school football team's season ended.
Who this is going to: Readers of the local paper's sports section.

sports column on the high school teams season after the playoff loss. facts: they went 8-2, lost in round 2 of state to a team they beat by 20 back in week 3, dez kowalski threw for 2340 yards this year which is a school record. my actual take: they lost because they abandoned the run game in the second half even though it was working, they were only down 3. give an actual opinion dont just recap the season, and dont pad it out with three examples for every point, some points just need one
essay.commentary.002:
About me: Real estate agent, 10 years working a mid-size Midwest suburb, writes an occasional commentary piece for her brokerage's blog.
Who this is going to: Brokerage blog readers and local paper real estate column readers.

commentary piece for the brokerage blog on the local market right now. facts: median days on market in my zip went from 8 days last spring to 34 this spring, list prices are still up 6% year over year even though showings per listing dropped by half, i personally have 3 sellers right now refusing to drop price after 60+ days on market. my actual take: this isnt a crash, its sellers who havent caught up to the new pace, the ones pricing it right are still selling in under 2 weeks. give a real opinion, dont hedge it into the market is complex and depends on many factors
essay.commentary.003:
About me: Fourth-generation soybean and corn farmer, writes an occasional commentary piece for the county farm bureau newsletter.
Who this is going to: County farm bureau newsletter readers; indirectly, state legislators.

commentary for the farm bureau newsletter about the prevented planting premium hike, usda raised the base rate 22% this year in our region. this spring i could only get 60% of my acres planted by the late may deadline because it wouldnt stop raining. the 22% hike costs me about 46k extra this year, and because of how the acreage caps work it hits family operations like mine harder proportionally than the bigger operations nearby. push people to support sb 214 which would fix the acreage cap. dont open with it is important to note that farming has always been unpredictable or anything like that, just get into the numbers
essay.coverletter.001:
About me: High-school biology teacher, 8 years, leaving teaching. Applying for a customer success role at an edtech company whose product she actually used in her classroom for two years.
Who this is going to: Hiring manager for Customer Success at the edtech company.

cover letter for a customer success job at the edtech company whose software i used for 2 years as a bio teacher. teaching = onboarding 30 kids a semester, handling angry parents, being the campus admin for their product. under 300 words, skip the 'i am writing to express' stuff
essay.coverletter.002:
About me: Former Army logistics staff sergeant, 12 years of service, honorably discharged and recently separated, applying for a civilian supply chain coordinator role.
Who this is going to: Hiring manager at Cascade Regional Distribution.

cover letter for a supply chain coordinator job at cascade regional distribution. my real experience: ran resupply convoys for an 800 soldier battalion across 3 bases, managed a 40 million dollar equipment account with zero loss on my last audit, redesigned our parts request process and cut fulfillment time from 11 days to 4. dont turn this into strong leadership and communication skills, use the actual numbers and what i actually did. under 300 words
essay.coverletter.003:
About me: IT helpdesk technician, 5 years at a mid-size hospital system, applying for a junior UX and product support role at the healthcare software company whose ticketing tool he uses at work every day.
Who this is going to: Hiring manager for the junior product and UX role at the software vendor.

cover letter for a junior ux role at the healthcare software company whose ticketing tool i use every single day at my helpdesk job. stuff to use: i field about 35 tickets a day, i built an internal wiki with 60+ troubleshooting articles that cut repeat tickets by about a third, and ive actually submitted 14 feature requests to their product team over the last 3 years and 4 of them shipped. keep this under 280 words hard cap, i dont want a wall of text
essay.newsletter.001:
About me: Owner and lead instructor of a small independent yoga studio, writes the monthly email newsletter to students herself.
Who this is going to: The studio's email list of current students.

intro paragraph for this months studio newsletter, keep it short this is just the intro not the whole newsletter. things to tease: new thursday 6am class starting, bonnies 10 year anniversary teaching here which were marking with a free saturday class, and the back parking lot reopens this week since the repaving is finally done. keep it under like 200 words, warm not corporate
essay.newsletter.002:
About me: Owner of a small independent hardware store, writes the seasonal email newsletter to customers himself.
Who this is going to: The hardware store's email list.

intro for our fall email newsletter to customers. tease: chainsaws and leaf blowers 15% off through the weekend, a free gutter cleaning safety demo saturday at 10am from a local roofer, and we finally restocked the kinco 901 gloves people keep asking about. write it as one paragraph like im talking to them, not some bulleted flyer list, keep it short its just the intro
essay.newsletter.003:
About me: Solo founder of Paperstack, a small invoicing app for freelancers, writes the monthly product newsletter to about 2,000 subscribers himself.
Who this is going to: Paperstack's newsletter subscribers.

intro for this months paperstack newsletter, keep it short its just the intro. things to mention: recurring invoices shipped last week, we fixed a bug that was double charging some stripe customers (want to be upfront about that one not bury it), and im doing free 15 min onboarding calls this month if people book one. sound like an actual person not a company, and please dont open with some in todays fast moving world of freelance work garbage
essay.opinion.001:
About me: Parent of two middle-schoolers in Millbrook, writing a letter-to-the-editor / op-ed for the local paper. Not a professional writer; has read the school board's proposal closely.
Who this is going to: Readers of the Millbrook Gazette; indirectly, the school board voting next month.

write a ~400 word op-ed for our local paper supporting the 8:45 school start time proposal. use the AAP sleep recommendation and the lincoln middle pilot results (attendance and first period grades went up). address the bus schedule objection (the proposal already re-tiers the routes). sound like a parent, not a policy paper
essay.opinion.002:
About me: Retired rural mail carrier who drove the same 38-mile Route 4 for 22 years before retiring last year in a small town called Kettle Falls. Writes occasional letters to the local paper but does not think of himself as a writer.
Who this is going to: Readers of the Kettle Falls Courier; indirectly, the USPS district manager attending the July 28 public forum.

letter to editor about the post office closing our counter service. i drove route 4 for 22 years so i know this town. usps says the branch loses 40k a year but thats only because they already cut the hours from 8 to noon, of course traffic dropped after that, theyre pointing at a problem they made themselves. mention mrs halloran on kettle hill, shes in her 80s and gets her heart meds by mail, she cant drive twenty minutes to fairview for a money order once the counter closes. push people to show up to the july 28 forum at the grange hall. keep it plain, im not a writer
essay.opinion.003:
About me: Girls' soccer and track coach at a public high school, writing an op-ed for the local paper. Coaches for the stipend, not because she wants to be a writer; direct and a little blunt.
Who this is going to: Readers of the local paper; the school board voting on next year's facilities budget.

op ed for the paper about getting turf on the girls soccer field. weve had 9 games rained out or moved the last two seasons bc the grass field floods, one of my girls tore her acl stepping in a rut last october, and the boys football field already got turf back in 2019 so this is also a fairness thing. dont open with some in todays world of youth sports garbage, just get into it
essay.personal.001:
About me: High-school senior applying to state universities. Has worked every weekend since sophomore year at her family's Vietnamese restaurant. Plain, honest writer when not trying to impress.
Who this is going to: College admissions readers.

college essay draft about working weekends at my family's restaurant. scenes to use: the saturday the register system died and i hand-wrote every ticket, teaching a new hire my grandma's spring roll fold, doing calc homework in the walk-in cooler on breaks. don't make it a lesson-learned essay, no 'little did i know', keep my voice plain
essay.personal.002:
About me: First-year nursing student, writing a personal essay for a scholarship application after her first clinical rotation in the ER. Plain-spoken outside of school writing.
Who this is going to: Scholarship committee readers.

essay for a scholarship app about my first week in the er. scenes: holding a strangers hand during a wound cleaning bc his wife was stuck in traffic, getting sent to restock gauze during a code and hearing them counting through the curtain, missing a blood draw 3 times on a diabetic guy with bad veins before finally getting it on the 4th try. keep my voice plain, i dont want it to sound like a thesaurus, no big fancy words
essay.personal.003:
About me: Retired Army staff sergeant, two deployments, now attends a veterans' writing workshop that publishes a small annual anthology. Writes plainly, not used to writing about himself.
Who this is going to: Readers of the veterans' writing workshop anthology.

personal essay for the vets writing workshop about coming home from my second deployment. scenes: standing in the cereal aisle for like 20 min bc there were suddenly 40 kinds of cereal and i couldnt pick one, my daughter hiding behind her moms leg at the airport bc she didnt recognize me at first, she was 6, and re learning to sleep with the bedroom door open after a year of sleeping facing the door out of habit. dont end it with some big meaning of it all paragraph, just tell it
essay.review.001:
About me: Remote software engineer who bought the Jarvik L2 standing desk 6 months ago and posts detailed reviews on her blog. Opinionated, specific, no affiliate links.
Who this is going to: Blog readers researching standing desks.

write my 6-month review of the jarvik L2 standing desk. quiet fast motor, wobbles at full height when i type hard, memory presets drift about a cm, cable tray is garbage. verdict: worth it at the $549 sale price, not at $749. be specific and opinionated, no fluff
essay.review.002:
About me: Home baker who sells sourdough at the farmers market on weekends and writes occasional gear reviews on her own baking blog.
Who this is going to: Blog readers who bake, deciding whether to buy the mixer.

review of the corlane sm6 stand mixer after 8 months, i use it almost daily for market sourdough. motor handles a double batch of stiff bagel dough with no stalling, bowl lift lever squeaks and i have to wd40 it once a month, the dough hook that comes with it is too short for the deep bowl so i bought a third party one, its 429 dollars which is 80 more than the equivalent kitchenaid. verdict: worth it if you knead heavy dough every week, skip it if youre mostly doing cookies and cake batter. be specific and give an actual verdict, dont hedge it into depends on your needs
essay.review.003:
About me: Owner-operator long-haul trucker, 14 months and about 140,000 miles on his current rig, posts short gear reviews for other owner-operators on a trucking forum.
Who this is going to: Other owner-operators on the trucking forum.

review of the roadstar dc4 dash cam, ive had it 14 months and put like 140k miles on it. rear cam night footage gets grainy past 30 feet, it caught a lane change accident on camera that got my insurance claim approved in 9 days instead of the usual 6 weeks, suction mount fell off twice in the summer heat till i switched to the adhesive mount, the app needs wifi to pull footage which is useless at most truck stops so i just pull the sd card instead. keep this under 350 words max, im not writing a novel about a camera
slack.channel.001:
About me: Store manager posting to the staff channel for her location, a mid-size retail chain. Casual team, nobody uses formal announcements.
Who this is going to: #store214 channel, 14 staff.

post to store214. starting mon july 20 weeknight closing shift moves from 9 to 830pm, corporate changed store hours. updated schedule is posted in the back office, flag me if it messes with anything. keep it like a normal staff note not some big formal announcement
slack.channel.002:
About me: IT and security lead at a software company, posting to #general about a mandatory password manager rollout.
Who this is going to: #general channel, whole company, about 80 people.

post to general. starting friday everyone needs 1password installed, migrate your saved passwords by end of month, IT sends invites today, questions in it-help. this is routine dont make it sound like a fire drill
slack.channel.003:
About me: Office manager at a multi-provider medical clinic, posting to the staff channel about a new patient intake protocol.
Who this is going to: #clinic-team channel, front desk and clinical staff, 20 people.

post to clinic team. starting tmrw front desk verifies insurance eligibility before checkin not after, we had a billing backlog last month from doing it after. laptops with the eligibility tool are already set up at both desks. dont do a whole windup just tell them the change
slack.decline.001:
About me: Product designer, calendar already wrecked this week, protective of maker time. Friendly with Alex.
Who this is going to: Alex, a PM on a sister team, who just DM'd an invite.
They wrote: "hey! threw 30 min on your calendar thursday to brainstorm onboarding ideas for the Q3 flow, that work?"

reply to alex declining thursday. ask him to drop his top 3 ideas here instead and i'll respond with sketches by friday eod. if that doesn't work we can grab time next week. keep it warm, 2-3 lines
slack.decline.002:
About me: Ops manager overseeing four locations for a hospitality group, calendar full this week running walkthroughs. Friendly with the recipient.
Who this is going to: Ramona, the group's marketing manager, who just DM'd an invite.
They wrote: "hey! grabbed 45 min weds at 2 to workshop the fall launch posts together, does that work?"

reply to ramona declining weds, im doing line checks at two locations that day. ask her to drop the draft posts here and ill comment by friday, can hop on a call next week if we need to. keep it short and warm
slack.delay.001:
About me: Data analyst, 2 years at the company. The quarterly churn report she owns is due to her manager tomorrow (Friday) morning.
Who this is going to: Her manager Priya: reasonable, hates surprises more than delays.
They wrote: "still good for the churn report tomorrow AM? want to skim it before the leadership sync at 2"

reply to priya. report slips to monday because the billing export was corrupted and finance had to rerun it. i can get her the topline churn number by noon tomorrow for her 2pm. own it, no essay
slack.delay.002:
About me: Account manager at a marketing and creative agency, owns the pitch deck for a Thursday new-business meeting with a furniture retailer prospect.
Who this is going to: Elena, her manager. Checks in early, does not like being blindsided the morning of.
They wrote: "how's the halden deck coming? want to review before we walk into thursday"

reply to elena. deck is getting rebuilt bc halden sent new brand guidelines late fri that contradict half of what we built. ill have a reviewable version to her by tmrw 5pm, still leaves time before thursday. own it dont over apologize
slack.delay.003:
About me: Program coordinator at a nonprofit housing organization, owns a grant report due to the executive director today for a foundation deadline.
Who this is going to: Rudy, the executive director. Reasonable, but wants a clean status update, not a narrative.
They wrote: "you still on track for the packard grant report today?"

reply to rudy. report slips to wed eod bc the county sent updated intake numbers this morning that dont match the draft, need to reconcile before it goes out. keep it short dont give me a whole backstory on the data partnership
slack.favor.001:
About me: Backend engineer on a 6-person platform team. It's Thursday afternoon; her sister's wedding is this weekend and she's on the on-call rotation.
Who this is going to: Marcus, teammate, same rotation, has swapped shifts with her once before (she covered his July 4th weekend).

slack dm to marcus asking him to cover my on-call this sat-sun for my sister's wedding. offer to take his nov 8-9 shift. i covered his july 4th so he owes me one but don't say it like that
slack.favor.002:
About me: Vet tech at a four-doctor animal clinic, six years in. It's Monday; her old dog needs a cardiology workup at a specialist two hours away, and Friday 1pm is the only slot open for the next six weeks.
Who this is going to: Teresa, fellow vet tech on the same rotation. They cover each other informally; Teresa took a Saturday shift for her back in the spring.

dm to teresa asking her to cover my fri 1-5 shift, my dog has a cardiology appt 2 hrs away and its the only slot for weeks. offer to take her sunday closing shift next week instead. i have to confirm the appt by eod tomorrow so mention that but dont make it sound pushy
slack.favor.003:
About me: Field coordinator at a construction and field-service company, five years in. It's Monday; the city just moved her Baker Ave permit inspection to Wednesday 7-9am, the exact window she's supposed to sign for a concrete delivery at the Elm St site.
Who this is going to: Mike, another field coordinator. They swap coverage often and don't keep score.

message mike, need him to sign for the concrete delivery wed morning 7-9am at elm st bc my permit inspection got moved to baker ave same window. just ask straight up dont bury it in a paragraph about the permit stuff
slack.groupsched.001:
About me: 3rd grade team lead at an elementary school, needs to lock a time this week for the three 3rd grade teachers to align on field trip logistics before Thursday.
Who this is going to: Group DM with Kevin and Priti, fellow 3rd grade teachers.
Context: "Kevin: cant do tuesday, i have car duty till 330"
Context: "Priti: wednesday after school works for me, or thursday morning before first bell"
Context: "Kevin: thursday morning works better for me actually, tuesday and weds are rough"

reply in the group dm. lock thursday morning 745 in my room before first bell, ask kevin and priti to confirm. dont recap the whole back and forth just land on the time
slack.groupsched.002:
About me: Engineering manager scheduling a 30-minute sprint planning make-up session since Monday's got cancelled for an all-hands.
Who this is going to: Group DM with three engineers: Charmaine, Jon, and Wale.
Context: "Charmaine: tuesday afternoon is out for me, doctor appt"
Context: "Jon: wednesday 10am or friday 2pm both work for me"
Context: "Wale: friday 2pm is bad, i have the vendor call, wednesday works"

reply in the group dm. wednesday 10am works for everyone based on what they said, lock it in and say ill send the invite after. dont list out who said what just land on the time
slack.missed.001:
About me: Caseworker at a nonprofit housing organization. Missed a message from a partner org two days ago about a shared client's housing appointment.
Who this is going to: Nadia at a partner shelter network, who sent the original message Saturday.
They wrote: "(sent 2 days ago) hey, can you confirm Malik T. is still good for the housing intake appointment weds at 10? need to lock the slot"

reply to nadia. sorry for the slow reply, was out over the weekend. malik is still good for weds 10am, let me know if you need anything else to lock it in. one clean apology not a bunch of sorries
slack.missed.002:
About me: Real-estate agent. Missed a message from a title company coordinator sent three days ago about a signature needed on a closing document.
Who this is going to: Farah at the title company.
They wrote: "(sent 3 days ago) need your signature on the amended closing disclosure for 118 Winslow before we can schedule closing, can you send it back today or tmrw?"

reply to farah. missed this, was traveling for another closing, signing it now and sending it back within the hour. one apology dont over explain the whole trip
slack.question.001:
About me: New marketing hire, week 2. Needs the Q2 campaign performance deck for a client call at 3pm today.
Who this is going to: Jordan, the marketing ops person who owns the drive folders. They've exchanged maybe five messages.

dm jordan asking where the q2 campaign performance deck lives in the drive, i need it for a 3pm client call. i'm new so be friendly but just ask, no long windup
slack.question.002:
About me: 5th grade teacher at a K-8 school, first year there. A field trip permission deadline is tomorrow and she needs the sign-out roster template.
Who this is going to: Nina, the front office admin who manages permission forms. They've spoken maybe three times.

dm nina asking where the field trip sign out roster template is, google drive or the binder in the office. need it by end of day tmrw to send to parents for thursdays trip. im new so friendly but just ask no big windup
slack.question.003:
About me: Store manager at one location of a mid-size retail chain. Wants to know if regional already ordered replacement POS terminals after last week's outage before she orders her own.
Who this is going to: Clyde, regional ops coordinator. She's messaged him maybe twice before.

message clyde asking if replacement pos terminals for store 214 are already on order from the outage ticket i filed last week, dont want to double order. put the actual question up front
slack.review.001:
About me: Backend engineer, opened a PR fixing a race condition in the webhook retry queue. Wants it merged before Thursday's on-call handoff.
Who this is going to: Sanjay, a teammate who usually reviews her PRs.

ask sanjay to review pr 482, fixes the race condition in the webhook retry queue. its small, like 60 lines. want it merged by thursday before oncall handoff. keep it to like 2 sentences its not a big deal
slack.review.002:
About me: Copywriter at a creative agency, drafted three subject line options for a client's back-to-school email campaign. Client call is tomorrow at 11am.
Who this is going to: Bianca, the creative director. Usually gives quick gut checks over Slack.

ask bianca to look at the 3 subject lines for the back to school campaign and just tell me which one she likes best before the 11am client call tmrw. dont need a whole critique just a pick. dont turn this into a formatted doc just ask her in chat
slack.review.003:
About me: Estimator at a construction and field-service company, put together a bid for a warehouse re-roofing job. Needs a second set of eyes before it goes out Wednesday morning.
Who this is going to: Gary, a senior estimator with 15 years at the company who catches pricing mistakes.

ask gary to check the fenwick ave reroof bid before i send it wed morning, mainly the labor hours and the membrane material markup, not confident on that line. dont write a huge explanation of the whole bid just point him at what to check
slack.standup.001:
About me: Frontend engineer on a team that does async standup in #eng-standup every morning. Team norm is 2-4 casual lines; nobody uses headers.
Who this is going to: #eng-standup channel, 8 teammates.

write my standup for #eng-standup. yesterday: shipped the invoice pdf fix, reviewed kai's pr. today: pairing with kai at 11 on export flow, then starting the datepicker bug. blocked: still waiting on staging access for payments sandbox, ticket 4412 with IT. match how people actually write standups, not a formatted report
slack.standup.002:
About me: SRE on a small platform team at a software company. Async standups post in #ops-standup every morning; team norm is two or three plain lines, nobody uses headers or checklists.
Who this is going to: #ops-standup channel, 6 teammates.

standup for ops-standup. yesterday: rotated the expiring tls cert on the ingest gateway, paired with angela on alerting noise cleanup. today: finish the alerting cleanup then start on the postgres replica lag thing. blocked: still need jill to approve a maintenance window for the second gateway cert, asked her friday. write it like people actually type standups not a report
slack.standup.003:
About me: Transaction coordinator at a real-estate brokerage, posts a daily update in the team's #closings channel. Team writes in short casual lines, no formatting, no headers.
Who this is going to: #closings channel, 5 agents plus the broker.

post my update in closings. yesterday got hendricks docs over to title, chased the castellanos inspection report. today following up with the lender on nakamura bc the appraisal came in low, and prepping the hendricks closing packet. no blockers, just flag hendricks closes thurs 10am at the title office if anyone wants to swing by. keep it casual like always not a formatted report
slack.urgent.001:
About me: On-call backend engineer, just got paged.
Who this is going to: Wes, a teammate who pinged in #incidents.
They wrote: "hey you around? checkout is throwing 500s for like 10% of requests, need eyes on this now"

reply to wes in incidents. im on it, checking payment service logs first since thats the most recent deploy, update in 10 min. this is genuinely urgent dont make it sound casual or slow it down
slack.urgent.002:
About me: Permits clerk at a city permits and inspections office.
Who this is going to: Trisha in the records office, who pinged urgently.
They wrote: "need the scanned copy of the Ferris Rd survey ASAP, applicant is standing at my counter and the file's not in the shared drive"

reply to trisha. i have the ferris rd survey, its on my desktop just mislabeled as final v2, sending it over right now. she needs this fast dont pad it out
slack.urgent.003:
About me: GM at a restaurant, two line cooks just called out for tonight's dinner service.
Who this is going to: Doug, her regional manager, who pinged asking for a status update.
They wrote: "heard two of your cooks called out, are you covered for tonight or do you need me to send someone from the Elm St location"

reply to doug. not covered, need one cook from elm st by 4pm, ill run the line myself till then. this is a real problem so dont undersell it or bury the ask
social.community.001:
About me: Community manager for Notepilot, a small note taking app with an active Discord server, about 4,200 members. Posts changelog updates in the announcements channel.
Who this is going to: Discord: Notepilot's server, mostly power users and people who filed feature requests.

discord announcement, offline mode is live, it was our top requested feature, 340 upvotes on the board. keep it casual like actually talking to the server not corporate changelog voice
social.community.002:
About me: Volunteer coordinator for the Elm Street Neighborhood Association, posts to their private Facebook group of about 850 members a few times a season.
Who this is going to: Facebook group: Elm Street neighborhood residents.

facebook group post, cleanup is oct 17 9am at elm and 4th, bring gloves, city is providing dumpsters this year which they didnt last year. keep it simple dont need a whole production
social.community.003:
About me: Board member for the Fairview Youth Soccer League, posts schedule and field updates to the parents' Facebook group.
Who this is going to: Facebook group: Fairview Youth Soccer League parents.

post to the parents group, north fields closed sat for resodding, games move to briar creek east fields same times. needs to be clear and short people dont read long posts
social.instagram.001:
About me: Owner and florist at Marigold and Vine, a small flower shop. Posting a photo of a cascading arrangement she built last weekend, dahlias and blackberry vine, for a barn wedding.
Who this is going to: Instagram followers: past and future wedding clients, local vendors.

ig caption for the photo of grace and omars wedding arrangement, dahlias and blackberry vine, barn venue. warm but not cheesy, one or two emoji max, no giant hashtag pile at the end
social.instagram.002:
About me: Owner of a small strength training gym, CrossFit adjacent, about 25 regular members. Posting a video of longtime member Dolores, 61, hitting her first unassisted pull up.
Who this is going to: Instagram followers: current members, local prospects.

caption for dolores's pull up video. shes 61, been training with us 14 months, started barely able to hang from the bar. proud of her but keep it real not cheesy hype
social.instagram.003:
About me: Owner and stylist at a small hair salon. Posting a before and after photo of a color correction, a client came in with brassy orange from box dye, it took 5 hours to get to a cool ash blonde.
Who this is going to: Instagram followers: current and prospective clients.

caption for the before after color correction pic, brassy orange box dye to ash blonde, took 5 hours. be honest its a big job not just a quick dye, and please no im obsessed swipe to see the magic type stuff
social.launch.001:
About me: Co-founder of Sprout, a two-person budgeting app for freelancers with irregular income. Launching publicly today on X. 900 followers.
Who this is going to: X: indie hackers, freelancers, early adopters.

launch tweet for sprout. it turns irregular freelance income into a steady monthly salary number you can actually budget on. we built it because we both freelanced and hated the feast/famine thing. one tweet, link at the end
social.launch.002:
About me: Event planner running Thistle and Table, a small seasonal supper club. Launching a new dinner series held in a friend's orchard, five courses built around whatever gets foraged that morning.
Who this is going to: Instagram and X followers: past supper club guests, local food folks.

launch post for the new foraged dinner series in the orchard. five courses, stuff foraged that morning, first one is first saturday in september, link at the end to book. one post not a whole essay
social.launch.003:
About me: Two person team launching Driftlist, a habit tracker built around two allowed skip days a month, so one missed day does not kill the whole streak. Launching on Product Hunt today.
Who this is going to: X followers: Product Hunt audience, indie hackers, early users.

launch tweet for driftlist. its a habit tracker where you get 2 skip days a month built in so one bad day doesnt kill your streak. we built it bc we both quit every other tracker after missing one day. link at the end. one tweet
social.launch.004:
About me: Owner of Smoke and Char BBQ, a food truck for four years, opening its first permanent walk up window.
Who this is going to: Instagram and X followers: regulars who follow the truck's schedule.

post announcing the walk up window opens july 25 at 214 5th st. means we can finally do the burnt ends sandwich we couldnt pull off out of the truck. one post keep it simple
social.linkedin_announce.001:
About me: Owner of Flour & Salt, a neighborhood bakery. Six years in, first location always packed on weekends. Not a LinkedIn person, but her business account has 800 followers, mostly locals and food-industry contacts.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn followers: locals, wholesale accounts, a few food journalists.

linkedin post announcing our second bakery location opening march 2 on maple street. rosa and jae are getting promoted to run it. proud but not cringe please
social.linkedin_announce.002:
About me: Owner of Ridgeline Landscape and Hardscape, a family run outdoor contracting company outside Denver, 11 years in business. About 1,200 LinkedIn followers, mostly local business owners, suppliers, and past clients. Not a frequent poster, maybe once a month.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn followers: local business contacts, suppliers, past clients, a couple of Golden city officials.

linkedin post, we won the bid to redo golden's town square, andre's running point on the crew, starts in september. proud of this one but keep it human not corporate speak
social.linkedin_announce.003:
About me: Owner and lead therapist at Northshore Physical Therapy, a three therapist outpatient clinic. About 600 LinkedIn followers, mostly referring doctors and former patients. Posts a handful of times a year.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: referring physicians, former patients, other clinic owners in the area.

linkedin announcement, we're launching a pelvic health program aug 1, mara just got certified and is running it. don't make it corny with the whole none of this would be possible without my team thing, just say the thing
social.linkedin_hiring.001:
About me: Owner of Crestview Heating and Air, an HVAC contractor with 14 employees in Columbus, Ohio. Posts occasionally, mostly hiring and job site photos.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: trade school grads, local job seekers, other contractors.

hiring post for an hvac apprentice, no experience needed, paid to train under owen, 40 hrs a week starting at 19/hr. sound like a person not indeed.com
social.linkedin_hiring.002:
About me: Practice manager at Willowbrook Animal Hospital, a small animal vet clinic. Posts on the clinic's LinkedIn a few times a year, usually hiring.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: veterinary technicians, vet tech program grads.

post about the overnight vet tech role we're opening. be honest that its a hard shift, some nights you're solo. dont just sell it like everything is amazing
social.linkedin_hiring.003:
About me: Co-founder of Ridgepoint, a three person SaaS tool that helps small trucking dispatchers schedule loads. Posts occasionally about the business, no big following, maybe 900 connections.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: potential support hires, a few customers who follow the founders.

hiring post, we're bringing on our first support person bc the 3 of us answering every ticket ourselves is falling apart. explain what the job actually is day to day, not just look how great our culture is
social.linkedin_lesson.001:
About me: In-house recruiter at a logistics company, 9 years in the field, ~4k LinkedIn followers. Posts twice a month, usually gets decent comments because the takes are specific.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: recruiters, hiring managers, job seekers.

linkedin post about something i've noticed in ~200 interviews: the best candidates ask about our last failed project. give one example of how that conversation goes. no fake stats, no listicle
social.linkedin_lesson.002:
About me: Real estate agent in Asheville, North Carolina, 12 years selling, mostly first time buyers. About 3,000 LinkedIn followers, posts once or twice a month with decent engagement because her stories are specific.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: buyers, other agents, a few local lenders.

linkedin post about something ive noticed after 12 years selling: buyers who walk the neighborhood at 7am before we tour end up way happier with the house later. tell the sycamore street story with the pruitts. no fake stats, no listicle, no weird one sentence per line thing
social.linkedin_lesson.003:
About me: Wedding photographer based in the Texas Hill Country, shoots about 35 weddings a year, 8 years full time. 5,400 LinkedIn followers, posts occasionally about the business side of photography.
Who this is going to: LinkedIn: engaged couples researching photographers, other wedding vendors.

post about how the couples who ditch the shot list always end up with the best photos. use the barrera wedding as the example, the one that got rained out and we moved everything under the pecan tree. no invented percentages please
social.x_post.001:
About me: Solo indie developer of Ledgerly, a personal-finance iOS app. Casual, self-deprecating X presence, 2,100 followers, posts build-in-public updates.
Who this is going to: X followers: indie hackers, iOS devs, some users.

tweet: ledgerly just passed 1000 paying users. took 14 months, first 100 took 9 of them. keep it casual, one tweet, no hashtags
social.x_post.002:
About me: Owner of Larkspur Farm Stand, a family produce stand in the Willamette Valley that also sells at two farmers markets. About 400 X followers, tweets casually about what's ripe.
Who this is going to: X followers: regulars, farmers market customers, a few other growers.

tweet: first tomatoes of the season came in, like 2 weeks early this year. casual, one tweet, no hashtags
social.x_post.003:
About me: Solo developer of Driftwood, a small narrative puzzle game, releasing a free demo on Steam. 1,800 X followers, posts build updates a couple times a week.
Who this is going to: X followers: indie game devs, players who wishlisted, a few streamers.

social.x_reply.001:
About me: Engineering manager at a 60-person company, 5 years of fully-remote teams. Posts occasionally, replies more.
Who this is going to: Reply to a viral post (40k likes) that says: 'Remote work killed mentorship. Juniors hired since 2020 are years behind where they should be. Offices exist for a reason.'
Context: "Remote work killed mentorship. Juniors hired since 2020 are years behind where they should be. Offices exist for a reason."

reply to this disagreeing. our juniors pair 2 hrs/day on a set schedule and they're strong. mentorship needs structure, not an office. one reply, don't write an essay, don't be smug
social.x_reply.002:
About me: Chef and owner of a 40 seat neighborhood restaurant in New Orleans, 6 years open. Posts occasionally, replies more when food industry topics go viral.
Who this is going to: Reply to a viral post (28k likes) that says: automatic gratuity is a scam, just pay your staff a real wage and end tipping entirely, restaurants that add service charges are hiding behind it.
Context: "automatic gratuity is a scam, just pay your staff a real wage and end tipping entirely. restaurants that add service charges are hiding behind it."

reply disagreeing. we actually tried the no tip flat 20 percent thing for 8 months and had to reverse it, couldnt compete hiring against tipped spots nearby. one reply, dont write an essay, dont be a jerk about it
social.x_reply.003:
About me: High school English teacher, 16 years teaching, mostly lurks on X, replies when education topics go viral.
Who this is going to: Reply to a viral post (55k likes) that says: kids today can't write a single paragraph without AI. The generation that grew up with chatgpt is going to be functionally illiterate by 25.
Context: "kids today can't write a single paragraph without AI. the generation that grew up with chatgpt is going to be functionally illiterate by 25."

reply pushing back on this. my students in class handwritten essays got better this year bc i moved all first drafts to timed and unplugged, the ai stuff actually forced me to fix my assignments. one reply, not defensive sounding, dont write a paragraph
